mnemonics.title: THRASHING CAUSES
mnemonics.content: CAUSE: COMPETING PROCESSES NEED FRAMES - Each process competes for limited frames. When frames are insufficient, processes cannot hold their working sets, causing constant page faults and thrashing.

mnemonics.title: SYMPTOMS OF THRASHING
mnemonics.content: HIGH PAGE FAULT + LOW CPU = THRASHING. When page faults are very high AND CPU utilization is very low, the system is thrashing. Remember: THRASHING = HIGH FAULTS, LOW CPU.

mnemonics.title: WORKING SET MEMORY
mnemonics.content: WINDOWS OF TIME: The working set depends on τ (tau), the time window. If window is too small, working set is too small and you'll thrash. If window is too large, you hold unnecessary pages.

mnemonics.title: CONTROL STRATEGIES
mnemonics.content: THREE CONTROLS: 1) Working Set - keep recent pages. 2) PFF - monitor fault rate. 3) Suspend - swap out processes. When in doubt, SUSPEND to reduce demand.

acronyms.usage: A thrashing control technique that monitors the page fault rate of each process and allocates or reclaims frames based on whether the rate exceeds upper or falls below lower thresholds.
A page replacement algorithm that evicts the page least recently used, helping keep actively used pages in memory and reducing thrashing.
A page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page in memory. Can contribute to thrashing if it evicts frequently used pages.
Same as FCFS - the oldest page in memory is replaced first. Simple but can cause thrashing by removing frequently used pages.
Page replacement strategy where each process is restricted to its own allocated frames, helping prevent one process from causing system-wide thrashing.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a curve showing CPU utilization (Y-axis) vs Degree of Multiprogramming (X-axis). It rises, peaks at optimal point, then drops STEEPLY into thrashing zone. Label the peak as 'Optimal' and the drop zone as 'Thrashing'.
Imagine a sliding WINDOW of time (τ). Within this window, mark all pages referenced - that's the working set. These pages MUST stay in memory to avoid thrashing.
Draw two horizontal threshold lines on a graph of page fault rate vs time. Above upper line = allocate MORE frames. Below lower line = reclaim frames. Between lines = just right.
Think of frames as chairs and processes as people. If you have 10 chairs and 20 people, everyone keeps standing up and sitting down (thrashing). Suspend some processes = let some people leave.

analogies.analogy: Imagine trying to work on 10 different books spread across 10 different tables, but you only have hands to carry ONE book at a time. You'd spend all your time running between tables carrying books, never actually reading anything. That's thrashing - too much demand, not enough capacity.

Think of your desk at work. You need your current projects, reference papers, and frequently used items within arm's reach (your working set). If someone takes half your desk space, you'd constantly have to fetch things from storage - you'd 'thrash' between fetching and working.

PFF is like a teacher monitoring how often students ask for help. If too many ask too often (high fault rate), the teacher gives them more resources. If they rarely ask (low fault rate), the teacher takes resources away to share with others.

Think of eating at a buffet with one plate. One person = great. Two = okay. Ten people with one plate = chaos - everyone is fighting for the plate, no one is eating. That's thrashing - too many processes, not enough memory.
